text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
South London and Maudsley NHS Foundation Trust  
Neurosciences Outpatient Department, Denmark Hill, London SE5 8AZ  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Michael Roberts, DOB: 14-07-1988, Hospital No: A937462 NHS No. 7485201936  
12 Bramble Close, Croydon, CR0 6PT  

Diagnoses: 

Seizure disorder under ongoing evaluation. 

I reviewed Michael Roberts in clinic today following reports of breakthrough events at work and at home. He works as a warehouse picker operating between tall racking and narrow aisles, with lifting tasks and pace varying by shift pattern. He and his supervisor report that the events occur across both early and late shifts without a consistent temporal trigger, though he notes episodes are more noticeable on busier days when the picking rate is high. He describes a brief warning of light-headedness and a sense of detachment, occasionally followed by loss of awareness and a short confusional period. There have been no clear injuries, though he has had to stop tasks mid-aisle on several occasions.

Current medicines include levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily, commenced six months ago and maintained at this dose for the past three months. He reports good adherence, taking doses before shifts. He has not used emergency medicines and none have been prescribed. There is no clear pattern with sleep or missed meals, though he sometimes delays breaks on busy shifts. Alcohol intake is low and unchanged. Family history is non-contributory.

Seizure activity by his diary entry currently averages nineteen per month, with clusters of two to three events on fast-paced picking days. Workplace first aid staff have documented brief post-event disorientation lasting up to five minutes. No tongue biting or incontinence has been recorded. He wears a wrist activity tracker; the timestamps roughly align with diary entries.

On examination today, neurological assessment was unremarkable. Blood pressure and glucose were normal. Review of prior MRI brain (report from last year) was non-diagnostic, and a routine EEG undertaken previously showed no clear epileptiform activity. I have requested an ambulatory EEG with event button to capture typical episodes during working hours and have asked him to continue the seizure diary with precise shift details (start time, aisle zone, lifting load, and pace rating).

We discussed practical risk reduction at work. He will alert his supervisor when experiencing the warning to step out to a safe, open area away from racking and lifting equipment, and to avoid ladder use on days with multiple warnings. He has informed occupational health and will provide them with this clinic summary. He remains fit to work with these precautions pending further test results.

Plan:
- Continue levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; no dose change today.
- Ambulatory EEG arranged within 4–6 weeks; correlate with diary and shift data.
- Routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC) to monitor therapy tolerance.
- Provide written event protocol to workplace first aid (already supplied today).
- Follow-up in 8–10 weeks, sooner if events escalate or if injuries occur.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Seizure activity by his diary entry currently averages nineteen per month

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit seizure frequency statements. The letter states: 'Seizure activity by his diary entry currently averages nineteen per month, with clusters of two to three events on fast-paced picking days.' This provides a clear numeric monthly frequency. No other frequency contradicts or exceeds this. Therefore, the highest frequency to report is 19 per month.
seizure_frequency_number: Seizure activity by his diary entry currently averages nineteen per month
